title: Tags.scm-driven Chunker (Rust + Python)
description: Commit 0bb22e2. Vendors upstream tree-sitter-rust and tree-sitter-python tags.scm pinned to commits 77a37472 and 26855eab. Chunker compiles Query once per language, walks matches, pairs @name + @definition.X, dedups by span preferring more-specific kinds. ATTRIBUTIONS.md tracks MIT licensing of vendored queries.

title: ADR: BSD-3-Clause license; vendored queries dual-distributed under MIT
description: Top-level LICENSE is BSD-3-Clause. License field set on both crate manifests. Vendored tags.scm files retain their upstream MIT licensing per ATTRIBUTIONS.md — they're dual-distributed: MIT for the queries' upstream copyright holder, BSD-3-Clause for the rest of code_index.

title: ADR: Store/Graph/Analyzer decoupling
description: Decision: Store trait is the durable boundary (chunk/edge CRUD + vector recall + manifest). Graph is in-memory, hydrated from Store. GraphAnalyzer operates on &Graph, never on Store. Two independent axes of replaceability — swap storage without touching analyzers, swap analyzers without touching storage. Sqlite is the initial Store impl; redb/lance/duckdb are future swap-out candidates kept in the design memo.

title: Sqlite-vec extension for vector recall at scale
description: Brute-force cosine over all embeddings in recall_top_k is fine up to ~tens of thousands of chunks. Beyond that, swap to sqlite-vec extension (feature-gated) for ANN. FreeBSD compatibility note: extension loading needs rusqlite's load_extension feature; verify before committing.

title: code_index: add tree-sitter-cpp and tree-sitter-ruby grammar support
description:
## Motivation

code_index currently chunks Rust + Python via tree-sitter. The next concrete use cases for the tool are both **outside** that grammar set:

- **theme** (`~/src/proprietary_thaddeus/theme/`) — 766k LOC across 4,893 C/C++ files, plus substantial Ruby alongside (per operator 2026-05-21). Real failure mode: naive file-by-file Read exploration of theme is economically infeasible (~$1k+ in token cost just to skim, and the context window doesn't hold it anyway). AST-aware chunking is the only sane access pattern.

- **forge-rs** (`~/src/forge-rs/`) — Rust, already in code_index's wheelhouse. But cross-referencing theme chunks ↔ forge-rs chunks for porter/auditor workflows requires both sides to be indexed at AST granularity.

This is also the missing piece for the broader Claude-orchestration risk-check work (see memory `risk_check_lease_architecture_2026_05_21`): theme is the design reference for the lease/capacity-envelope architecture, and a porter agent can't reason about theme without AST-aware navigation.

## Scope

Add tree-sitter grammars for:

1. **C** (`tree-sitter-c`) — for theme's headers and any C sources
2. **C++** (`tree-sitter-cpp`) — for theme's main implementation
3. **Ruby** (`tree-sitter-ruby`) — for theme's Ruby alongside

For each language, implement:

- Grammar binding in the language-detector branch (extension-based: .c/.h, .cpp/.cc/.cxx/.hpp/.hh, .rb)
- Definition-boundary extraction rules — what counts as a "chunk" for this language:
  - C/C++: function definitions, struct/class/enum declarations, typedef, top-level template declarations, namespace blocks (probably chunk per-member rather than per-namespace)
  - Ruby: class/module definitions, def methods (instance + class), top-level constant assignments
- Symbol extraction (for the graph layer): function/method names, type names, references in call sites
- Test fixtures: one small C++ file, one small Ruby file, with expected chunk shapes

## Out of scope

- Refactoring code_index's internals beyond what's needed to add languages cleanly
- Performance optimization for large indexes (theme will surface real perf concerns, but address those as a follow-up if measured)
- Header-vs-source deduplication for C++ (chunking each file independently is fine for v1; the graph layer should naturally link them via reference edges)

## Acceptance

- `code-index ingest ~/src/proprietary_thaddeus/theme` completes and produces an index with chunks for the C/C++/Ruby files (not just skipped-due-to-unknown-language)
- `code-index recall "Shares::request"` (or similar) returns AST-bounded chunks containing that symbol, not full files
- `code-index ingest ~/src/forge-rs` continues to work (Rust regression test)
- New per-language tests in code_index's test suite pass under `cargo test`
- README updated with the new language list

## Composes with

- mu-ks1f (the measurement bead) — theme + forge-rs becomes the concrete benchmark for "does pre-load reduce cold-repo turn count?"
- Future: orchestration-as-API adapter design (see synthesis doc `~/.claude-personal/notes/risk-check-design-2026-05-21.md`) wants `observe_codebase(query)` as a framework verb — code_index is its implementation. Multi-language support is a precondition.

title: MCP as mu extensibility surface: Roots, Sampling, Completions
description:
Capture three MCP protocol features that change how mu components interact:

## Roots (immediate: code-index db resolution)
MCP Roots lets the server ask the client what directories it's working in. Replaces the current rootUri/cwd/env-var heuristics for code-index db resolution with a protocol-native mechanism. Server calls context.peer.list_roots(), maps each root to ~/.cache/code_index/<basename>.db. on_roots_list_changed notification keeps it current when the client switches projects.

## Sampling (architectural: sub-agent dispatch via mailbox)
Server asks the client LLM to run a completion — flips control so the tool becomes a coordinator that borrows the client brain. For mu mailbox: an MCP server could ask the client to reason about which session to route a message to, or to synthesize/summarize before returning. Alternative to explicit sub-agent spawn — the MCP server IS the orchestrator and the client IS the worker.

## Completions (UX: dynamic menus without frontend changes)
MCP Completions provides auto-complete suggestions for tool arguments. The server becomes the source of truth for what is available — the frontend just renders. For code-index: tab-complete on repo names, symbol names, context-aware suggestions. For mu-solo: slash menu backed by MCP completions (/recall <tab> shows indexed repos). New capabilities surface without TUI code changes.

## Implications for mu-solo
If mu-solo TUI is a thin renderer over MCP surfaces, adding features means adding MCP tools/completions — not changing the frontend. The TUI becomes a generic MCP client with rendering opinions, not a hardcoded menu system.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Tasks (SEP-1319) for orchestration

rmcp supports long-running tool invocations via #[task_handler]. Annotate tools with execution(task_support = "required" | "optional") and the SDK generates enqueue_task, tasks/list, tasks/get, tasks/result, tasks/cancel.

Natural fits:
- code_recall with semantic embeddings (external API call, non-trivial latency)
- Agent task dispatch: "run this sub-agent work" returns a task ID immediately, client polls or gets notified on completion
- Batch operations: reindex a repo, run a measurement suite

This is the mailbox pattern with protocol-level support. The MCP server becomes the task queue; the client is the scheduler. No hand-built polling — the protocol handles lifecycle.

Reference: rmcp examples/servers_task_stdio + examples/clients_task_stdio

title: ADR: Two-pass ingest then embed
description: ingest() does AST chunking + persistence only. embed_pending(store, embedder, batch_size) walks chunks lacking an embedding (via Store::chunks_missing_embedding NOT EXISTS query) and embeds in batches. Avoids borrow-checker tangle of passing &mut Store to both ingest and a sink, and makes embedding restartable after partial failure.

title: Surface matched-tokens / rank-source explanation in recall output
description:
Observed 2026-05-09 on jj_lseg: query 'reading parquet from S3' anchored on the high-frequency 'S3' token, returned mediocre results. Adding 'boto3' (a rare/specific token) gave significantly better results.

User-facing improvement: when a recall result is shown, optionally indicate WHY it ranked — which tokens matched lexically (with their per-token contribution from BM25 IDF), and where it ranked in semantic vs lexical (so the user can see 'this hit is purely from semantic similarity' vs 'this anchored on token X').

Implementation sketch:
  a. Track per-source rank in the Hit struct (sem_rank: Option<usize>, lex_rank: Option<usize>).
  b. For lexical: also return matched tokens (FTS5 supports this via highlight()/snippet() functions).
  c. CLI --explain flag prints a per-hit explanation block: 'matched [parquet, boto3]; semantic rank 7; lexical rank 2; combined RRF score 0.0451'.

Lower priority than at-n80 — this is a UX win for advanced users, not a capability gap. But the empirical signal is real: users will hit this.

title: Validation: pi_agent_rust ingest+recall yields useful results
description:
2026-05-09: code-index ingested all 32494 chunks across 1755 files of pi_agent_rust + embedded via qwen/qwen3-embedding-8b. Concurrent path delivered 6-7x speedup over sequential (73 peak chunks/s vs 9.4/s baseline). Recall queries return semantically-appropriate code blocks per Thaddeus's first-look feedback ('shows the appropriate blocks'). The tool earns its keep for the orientation/refactoring-impact use cases the design memo (memory 23c001fe) targeted.

Outcome: code_index v0.1 is functional end-to-end and validated on a real >32k-chunk codebase. Open work (at-n80 edge extraction, at-f5y oversized-chunk handling, at-dxk more languages, at-3sq sqlite-vec for scale) is incremental refinement, not blocking utility.

title: Add Go / JavaScript / TypeScript chunker languages
description: Vendor upstream tags.scm + add tree-sitter-* deps for go, javascript, typescript. ~30 min per language. Driven by what we want to scan; defer until we have a target codebase that needs them.

title: Skip or split oversized chunks in chunker
description:
Surfaced ingesting pi_agent_rust 2026-05-09: 154 chunks > 16KB, 21 > 64KB, 3 > 256KB, max 810KB (a 'tests' module captured as a single @definition.module chunk in src/extensions.rs). Chunks that large are bad retrieval units (too coarse to be specific) AND hit embedder token limits.

Options:
  a. Skip @definition.module captures over a length threshold — they're rarely useful retrieval units anyway.
  b. Split oversized definitions into smaller chunks (e.g. one per inner function for big modules).
  c. Hard-cap chunk text at chunker time (lossy but bounded; pairs with the EMBED_CHAR_LIMIT cap on the embed-input side).

Current state: embed input is capped at 24k chars (EMBED_CHAR_LIMIT) so embedding survives, but the stored chunk text is still the full huge body — wasteful and the recall won't surface the parts past 24k correctly even though they're stored.

Option (a) is the smallest change with the most signal-to-noise improvement. Probably the right v1.
close_reason: Implemented as commit ba8aeee. Module chunks > 32KB dropped at chunker time. Empirical: pi_agent_rust DB shrank 621MB → 481MB (23% reduction); 53 oversize module chunks → 0. All inner items still captured as their own chunks. Migration note for existing DBs in commit message.

title: Embedder + recall verb (two-pass pipeline)
description: Commit 4d2c2cd. Embedder trait, MockEmbedder (SHA256-derived deterministic vectors for tests), OpenRouterEmbedder (env key + config.toml fallback, 401 retry), select_embedder() for offline fallback. Two-pass: ingest then embed_pending(store, embedder, batch_size). recall verb returns ranked (id, score, optional chunk) hits via Store::recall_top_k.

title: Adaptive concurrency / 429 backoff in embed_pending_concurrent
description:
Observed 2026-05-09 ingesting jj_lseg with --embed-concurrency 16 against qwen/qwen3-embedding-8b: peak 114/s for opening burst, then stalls and stuttering recovery (6→15/s) as workers fought OpenRouter's rate limit. Sustained throughput at 16 was no better than at 8 (~60-80/s). All 16 workers hammered the rate-limit ceiling without coordination.

Improvement: detect rate-limit signals and back off cooperatively. Two complementary mechanisms:
  a. Retry with exponential backoff on HTTP 429 responses. ureq surfaces these as Err(Status(429, _)).
  b. Adaptive concurrency: track recent error rate; if >threshold, drop active workers; if 0 errors for N batches, allow more.

Or simpler: a global token-bucket rate limiter sized for the upstream's published limit. Less smart, but predictable.

Practical effect: smoother throughput at high concurrency without manual tuning per upstream. The 'right' --embed-concurrency value would stop being a thing the user has to figure out per provider.

title: Networkx subprocess GraphAnalyzer impl
description: Alternative GraphAnalyzer using a Python subprocess that loads networkx. JSON over stdin/stdout. Slower than petgraph by ~10x for big graphs but pulls in networkx's full algorithm library for free. networkx is 100% pure Python and runs cleanly on FreeBSD with optional numpy/scipy via ports. Caller picks via --analyzer flag.

title: Code-graph viz / report (deferred until after RTX 4000 lands)
description: graphify-style HTML graph viz and markdown report. Defer until after the RTX PRO 4000 Blackwell delivers (~2026-05-14, agent memory 94fa85a1) so LLM-inferred edges (the 43% of graphify edges that are model-reasoned, not AST) are free via local inference. Without local inference each repo refresh costs real API tokens; with local it's electricity.

title: Recall: down-weight test chunks for natural-language queries
description:
Observed 2026-05-09 dogfooding pi_agent_rust against flywheel-3p4 (slash-command dispatch bug):

Query 'Unknown command dispatch error message' returned tests as top 2 hits (shared_dispatch_unknown_tool_returns_invalid_request, rpc_errors_on_unknown_command_and_missing_params) before any actual dispatcher code. Tests have descriptive prose-style names + docstrings that match natural-language queries strongly; real source chunks have terse identifier names that match weakly.

Mitigations to consider:
  a. --exclude-tests flag: filter out chunks where kind=Test or filename matches /tests?/ patterns.
  b. Kind-weighted recall: at fusion time, multiply scores by a per-kind factor. Tests at 0.7, real source at 1.0 by default.
  c. Show kind in default output so users can spot test-domination: 'Test fn name | file' vs 'Method name | file'.
  d. --mode lexical helped here — pure lexical doesn't have this bias as strongly because BM25 IDF down-weights common test-style tokens.

Lower priority than at-qxy/at-hrg but worth filing while the empirical evidence is fresh.
notes: Implemented in commit 151ae9f. Added RecallTuning with test_penalty (default 0.5) and exclude_tests. looks_like_test() detects via ChunkKind::Test, name prefix (test_/should_), path component (tests/test/__tests__/spec/specs), filename suffix (_test.X/.test.X/.spec.X/_spec.X). recall_tuned() over-pulls 4x when filtering, applies post-RRF kind-aware multiplier. CLI: --no-test-penalty, --exclude-tests. 5 new tests; 66/66 pass. Verified on flywheel-3p4 query: 2 test-dir tests demoted, 2 production types promoted. Limitation: inline 'mod tests {}' tests in src/ files still leak — follow-up filed.

title: Scaffold code_index crate (types, traits, CLI verbs)
description: Initial commit da9b8c4. Defines Chunk, Edge, ChunkId, ChunkKind, EdgeKind, Store trait, GraphAnalyzer trait, Graph type. CLI verbs (clap-derive). Builds clean; bodies todo!().

title: Edge extraction from @reference.X tag captures
description:
Refined design after Thaddeus's question about whether tree-sitter already provides the association info: yes, three layers.

LAYER 1 — Parent traversal (free): Node::parent() / start_byte / end_byte give us 'which @definition.X span contains this @reference.X' without byte-range math. The tree carries it natively.

LAYER 2 — locals.scm (scope-aware references): tree-sitter ships a third standardized query format alongside tags.scm and highlights.scm. @local.scope, @local.definition.X, @local.reference with semantic scope awareness — handles shadowing, lexical scope, function params as locals. helix's runtime/queries/<lang>/locals.scm files use this for in-buffer 'go to definition'. We can use it for intra-file edges with HIGH confidence (1.0).

LAYER 3 — tags.scm @reference.X for unresolved (free) references: the references locals.scm couldn't bind. Best-effort by-name match against other chunks (intra-file other-defs or cross-file global). Medium-low confidence.

Implementation order:
  a. Vendor locals.scm for rust and python from upstream (same pattern as tags.scm — pinned commit shas, MIT, ATTRIBUTIONS).
  b. Run locals.scm → produce intra-file resolved Edges directly.
  c. Run tags.scm @reference.X → for unresolved refs, parent-walk to find containing @definition.X (intra-file) AND best-effort name lookup across the store (cross-file).
  d. Persist edges with confidence reflecting layer.

This is much better than 'best-effort name match for everything.' Upstream tree-sitter community has already encoded scope analysis per-language in locals.scm; reimplementing it would be NIH.
close_reason:
Landed Layer 3 (tags.scm @reference.X + name-based resolution) as commit 6894439. Layer 2 (locals.scm scope-aware) deferred — filing follow-up.

End-to-end on pi_agent_rust: 274172 references found, 119874 edges emitted, 78066 distinct after UNIQUE collapse. Top incoming-edge nodes (len/join/format/clone/iter) are Rust idioms — sensible. 70 sec for 1755 files.

title: Ingest verb (walk + chunk + persist + manifest)
description: Commit 45d38f5. walker (recursive with vcs/build skips), ingest (FNV-1a per-file signature → skip if unchanged, otherwise drop prior chunks via FK CASCADE and re-chunk), CLI body for code-index ingest. End-to-end: code-index ingest /path runs the full pipeline and is idempotent.

title: Petgraph-native GraphAnalyzer impl
description: First concrete GraphAnalyzer impl: pure Rust via petgraph for centrality (PageRank, betweenness) and shortest_path. Community detection via leiden-rs or label-propagation. Wires to graph build/communities/path/centrality CLI verbs.

title: Recall: detect inline 'mod tests {}' blocks within source files
description:
Follow-up to at-ix1 (commit 151ae9f). The test down-weighting in recall_tuned() relies on three signals: ChunkKind::Test, name prefix (test_/should_), and path components. Inline test modules — e.g. 'mod tests { #[test] fn shared_dispatch_unknown_tool_returns_invalid_request() {...} }' inside src/extensions.rs — bypass all three:

1. The inner #[test] fn IS captured as ChunkKind::Test (so the kind-based penalty applies). But...
2. The names of inline tests often DON'T start with test_ — they're descriptive. The function-name-prefix heuristic misses these.
3. The path is src/extensions.rs — no 'tests' component. The path heuristic misses these.
4. The parent 'mod tests' chunk is dropped by at-f5y oversize-module filter, so we have no parent context.

So inline-mod-tests fall through ALL detection rules and get the un-penalized score.

Two paths forward:

A. Track parent module context per chunk. Add a 'parent_module' field; populate during chunker walk. Then looks_like_test() can check parent_module == 'tests'. Cost: schema change, chunker walk-up logic, but cheap afterward.

B. Use #[test] attribute presence as a signal. A chunk with #[test] above is unambiguously a test regardless of name/path. Either capture attributes during chunker pass or grep raw chunk text for '#[test]' / '#[tokio::test]' / '#[rstest]'.

B is the more general and correct fix. A is cheaper but only catches Rust 'mod tests' convention.

Empirical: query 'Unknown command dispatch error message' on flywheel-3p4 still ranks shared_dispatch_unknown_tool_returns_invalid_request at #1 even with at-ix1's tuning. It's the ONLY test that survived demotion — and it's an inline-mod-tests fn.

title: Layer 2 edge resolution: locals.scm scope-aware references
description:
Refinement to at-n80's Layer 3. Currently we resolve @reference.X by name lookup against the chunks table — when multiple chunks share a name (e.g. 'len' is a method on dozens of types in pi_agent_rust), we pick same-file first then arbitrary, with confidence 0.85/0.6. Real precision requires scope analysis: shadowing, function parameters, lexical scope.

Tree-sitter has a third standardized .scm format alongside tags.scm: locals.scm. Captures @local.scope, @local.definition.X, @local.reference. Helix's runtime/queries/<lang>/locals.scm files use it for in-buffer 'go to definition'.

Implementation:
  a. Vendor locals.scm for rust + python from upstream tree-sitter repos (same MIT pattern as tags.scm).
  b. Run locals.scm query during build_edges → get scope tree + intra-scope name resolution. High confidence (1.0) for these.
  c. tags.scm @reference.X remains the fallback for whatever locals.scm couldn't resolve (cross-scope / cross-file).

Gauge value before doing this: run the v1 build, compute fraction of multi-candidate resolutions, compute how often the same-file heuristic picks the right answer (sample manually). If <80% precision on multi-candidate names is hurting actual recall, do this. Otherwise defer.

title: ADR: tags.scm reuse from upstream tree-sitter-* repos
description: Per-language extraction config comes from upstream tree-sitter-<lang>/queries/tags.scm files (MIT, vendored under code_index/src/chunker/queries/<lang>/, pinned to commit shas in ATTRIBUTIONS.md). Same format helix uses. We avoid reimplementing per-language case analysis; we lift the standardized github-tree-sitter-tags queries directly. Span dedup with kind precedence handles the impl-method-vs-function overlap idiom in tree-sitter-rust.

title: Parallelize embedding batches (currently sequential, sbwait-bound)
description:
Observed 2026-05-09 ingesting pi_agent_rust: ~31k chunks @ batch_size=16 = ~1900 sequential HTTP round-trips to OpenRouter. Process pegs in sbwait (FreeBSD socket-buffer-wait); CPU/memory near-idle. Total real time scales linearly with batch count.

Improvement: issue N batches concurrently via a worker pool (rayon for the simple case, or asupersync if we follow the pi-rust runtime convention). Speedup is linear up to OpenRouter's per-key rate limit (likely ~10-50 RPS for embeddings).

Bonus: add a progress indicator so long ingests don't look hung. ureq's blocking nature means stdout stays silent for minutes. Even a 'embedded N of M' line every 100 batches would be welcome.

Defer until we know the typical workload — for codebases under ~5k chunks the current sequential path is fine; pi_agent_rust at 31k+ is the threshold where it stops feeling acceptable.
close_reason: Implemented via std::thread::scope rather than asupersync (per Thaddeus's preference to not commit to async stack yet). Per-worker channels for round-robin distribution, sequential persistence on main thread to avoid sqlite write contention. Default concurrency=8 batches in flight. Includes progress indicator (was a separate observed need; landed together since they touched the same code path). Commit 432ac46.

title: restore should clear supersession fields (superseded_by/supersede_reason) and close/annotate the edge
description: Found reversing the first live sweep's flagged edge (754bc38b): restore sets lifecycle=active but leaves superseded_by pointing at the no-longer-superseding memory. Read paths key off lifecycle so it's latent, not live — but a later supersession silently overwrites history and the FK lies to anyone reading the row raw. Also consider a 'sweep-reversal' kind or status on the supersessions row. Spec ref: bead-plan/recommendation in mu/.delegations/overnight-2026-06-12/RESULTS-fable5/.

title: sweep follow-ups: case-insensitive prose markers + dry-run queue tally fix
description: From the first live sweep: (1) PROSE_MARKERS matching uses instr() (case-sensitive) — caught 45 of ~123 prose-marked memories; use lower()/nocase. (2) dry-run summary counts 'queued' substring which 'would-queue' doesn't contain — queue tally reads 0 in dry-run output.

title: SqliteStore impl + BSD-3-Clause LICENSE
description: Commit 0042998. First Store impl: rusqlite bundled, WAL, FK ON DELETE CASCADE, schema_version migrations. Brute-force cosine over LE-bytes BLOB embeddings for top-K (no sqlite-vec yet). Adds LICENSE.

title: Hybrid retrieval — pair semantic recall with BM25/lexical signal
description:
Pure-semantic recall via qwen/qwen3-embedding-8b has known holes — short queries against ~5k+ chunks dilute signal, exact-keyword matches lose to close-but-not-exact semantic neighbors, and code chunks where the relevant signal is one line inside a 200-line function rank too low even when the embedding correctly recognized similarity.

Concrete observation 2026-05-09 on jj_lseg ingest: 'reading parquet from S3' query returned fewer than expected locations. Comparable queries on pi_agent_rust felt sharper.

The well-known fix: hybrid retrieval — combine semantic embedding similarity with lexical (BM25 over inverted index, or a simpler tantivy index, or even rust's regex against names+text). Frankensearch's whole architecture is this two-tier hybrid (memory 0d26b6f8). Graphify pairs both implicitly.

Implementation sketch:
  a. Add a tantivy index over (name, text, file) per chunk during ingest. ~1-2 days.
  b. recall walks both: top-K semantic + top-K lexical, fuses via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), returns merged ranked list.
  c. Optional: --mode {semantic, lexical, hybrid} flag for testing each individually.

Stop-gap that requires no new deps: at recall time, also do a substring search across chunks.text for the query terms and merge with semantic rank. Cheaper but coarser.
close_reason: Implemented as commit 31d293b. SQLite FTS5 over chunks(name, text) added as V2 schema migration with auto-rebuild on existing DBs. New Store::recall_lexical method, recall::RecallMode enum, recall_with_mode orchestrator using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF, Cormack 2009 formula with k=60). CLI gets --mode {hybrid,semantic,lexical}, default hybrid. Empirical confirmation against jj_lseg: 'reading parquet from S3' query now returns chunks (1849, 3581) that semantic-only had missed entirely. 45 lib tests pass.

title: Dedup embed.rs into shared agent_tools/embed workspace crate
description: code_index/src/embed.rs is structurally duplicated from agent_tools/agent/src/embed.rs (~150 line overlap: Embedder trait, MockEmbedder, OpenRouterEmbedder, config.toml fallback, vector serialization helpers). Extract into agent_tools/embed crate as workspace member. Both agent and code_index depend on it. Defer until a third consumer makes the dedup payoff; until then keep the two surfaces drift-compatible. Source comment in embed.rs already flags this.

title: Real-embedder smoke test with OpenRouter against forge-rs
description: All tests so far use MockEmbedder (deterministic but semantically meaningless). Need to validate that recall actually returns relevant chunks against a real embedder. Plan: source OPENROUTER_API_KEY from ~/.config/agent/config.toml via tq, run code-index ingest /path/to/forge-rs, then a few targeted recall queries (e.g. 'ownership transition', 'borrowed buffer', 'into_owned'). Verify ranked hits make semantic sense. Cost: ~few cents at qwen3-embedding-8b rates.
close_reason:
Validated semantic recall against forge-rs with qwen/qwen3-embedding-8b via OpenRouter. 44 chunks indexed; targeted queries return semantically relevant hits (not just string-match):

  'promote borrowed to owned' → into_owned_promotes_borrow_to_owned (0.82),
    into_owned_promotes_borrowed_to_owned_with_distinct_storage (0.80),
    into_owned_passes_through_owned (0.72)

  'comparing buffers for equality' → equality_of_two_default_buffers_is_defined_and_true (0.74),
    content_equality_ignores_ownership_and_checks_size_and_bytes (0.69)

Cost: ~22k tokens at qwen3-embedding-8b rates (~$0.0002). Pipeline confirmed end-to-end.

title: adjudicator: sanitize control chars in model verdict JSON before parse
description: Observed live 2026-06-12 18:12Z on a real memory add: 'adjudication skipped (fail-open): parsing verdict array: control character (\u0000-\u001F) found while parsing a string at line 5 column 0'. Fail-open held (memory saved, no adjudication). Fix: strip/escape control chars from the model's verdict text before serde parse, or use a lenient parse; add a test with embedded control chars. Component: agent/src/adjudicate.rs (shipped PR #16).

title: MCP server mode for sub-agent context preloading
description:
code-index serve as an MCP server so sub-agent dispatch can ask 'give me the N most relevant chunks for query X' without invoking the binary. The use case the whole tool was designed for. Likely needs a tokio-or-asupersync runtime; pi-rust is the precedent for the latter on FreeBSD.

## 2026-05-26 findings (LSP experiment)

Tried wiring code-index-lsp as a Claude Code LSP plugin via socat bridge (stdio→TCP:10.1.1.172:7621). The LSP protocol works end-to-end — workspace/symbol returns ranked results from the indexed DB. However, Claude Code's LSP tool interface doesn't expose a free-text query parameter for workspaceSymbol — it extracts the query from the word under the cursor at file:line:character. This makes it useless for exploratory search ("find SessionEventLog across repos") since you'd need to already have the symbol in a file to search for it.

**Conclusion:** code-index belongs as an MCP tool, not an LSP server for Claude Code. The tool description should position it as "reach for this before grep when searching across repos" — a search tool, not a diagnostic tool. The server (code-index-lsp at 10.1.1.172:7621) is running and functional; the socat bridge at ~/.local/bin/code-index-lsp-bridge works. The MCP adapter just needs to wrap the recall API.

**Also fixed:** resolve_db_for_root now checks db_is_populated() (file size >= 100KB) instead of just exists(). Empty per-project .code_index/index.db files no longer shadow the populated ~/.cache/code_index/<name>.db databases. Binary deployed to ~/pot-bin/code-index-lsp.
